section.para: The USCO's position on prompts becomes even more tenuous when viewed alongside the parallel legal debate over the use of copyrighted materials in AI training datasets. Copyright holders argue that the scraping and copying of their works to train models is mass infringement, while AI developers counter that this is a "transformative" fair use because the models are learning statistical patterns, not the expressive content itself.5

The USCO's emerging guidance on this issue creates a logical contradiction. In its May 2025 report, the Office suggests that training AI models is not always fair use, particularly when the AI's output could harm the market for or substitute the original works it was trained on.7 This implies that the ingestion and processing of training data
does implicate the expressive, copyrightable elements of the source material.
Herein lies the paradox: The USCO's position requires believing two contradictory things simultaneously.

listitem.para: On Input (Training): An AI model's ingestion of a copyrighted book is considered a reproduction of its creative expression, which may constitute infringement.

On Output (Prompts): A human's detailed instructions to that same model are considered a mere idea, and the resulting output is not a product of human expression because the AI made the final choices.

section.para: This is an inconsistent and unsustainable legal posture. The generative AI model is a complex process that transforms expressive input (training data) into expressive output (the generated work). The USCO cannot logically argue that the front end of this process involves copyrightable expression while the back end, initiated by a human's specific and creative instructions, does not. This inconsistency appears to be an attempt to penalize AI developers for their training methods while simultaneously denying copyright protection, and thus economic incentive, to the human creators who use these tools.9
